content:
# Leviticus Chapter 10 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 63r–63v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_010_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_010_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 94–95 | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_010_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_010_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 10 is the immediate sequel to the theophanic climax of chapter 9 — the divine fire that consumed the offerings and caused the people to shout and fall on their faces (9:24). Chapter 10 opens with a second fire from the LORD (10:2), but this one is not an act of acceptance: it is an act of judgment. The structural movement is deliberate and terrible: divine fire accepts the legitimate worship of the installed priests (ch.9) → divine fire destroys the illegitimate worship of two of those priests (10:1-2). The chapter divides into three panels: (1) vv.1-7: the incident of Nadav and Avihu — unauthorized fire, divine consumption, Moshe's theological interpretation, removal of the bodies, and prohibition on mourning for Aharon's family; (2) vv.8-11: the divine prohibition on wine and strong drink for priests on duty, with theological rationale; (3) vv.12-20: Moshe's instructions on eating the holy portions, and the crisis of the uneaten sin-offering goat resolved by Aharon's defense. The Hebrew of ch.10 is terse and charged throughout, with a concentration of legal and theological vocabulary unique in the Pentateuch. MT and English versification are identical.]*

---

**1** And the sons of Aharon [u-vney Aharon] — Nadav [Nadav] and Avihu [Avihu] — each took his fire-pan [vayiqechu ish machtato] [machtah — "fire-pan / censer"; a portable metal vessel used to carry burning coals; the same term used for the gold censers of the tabernacle service (Ex 25:38; Num 4:9) and for the censers of Korach's company (Num 16:6); in the priestly context the machtah is the vessel by which fire is transported from the altar of burnt offering to the altar of incense for the twice-daily incense offering (Ex 30:7-8)] and they put fire in them [vayittenu bahen esh] and placed incense upon it [vayasimu aleha qetoret] and brought near before the LORD [vayaqrivu lifney YHWH] esh zarah [esh zarah] [esh zarah — "strange fire / unauthorized fire / foreign fire"; the precise nature of the transgression has been debated since antiquity. The adjective zarah (from the root zur, "to be strange, foreign, unauthorized") is the same root used for the "unauthorized person" (zar) who is prohibited from approaching the altar (Num 1:51; 3:10, 38). "Strange fire" is fire not authorized by the LORD's command — most likely either (a) fire taken from a source other than the altar of burnt offering (cf. Lev 6:12-13, which commands that the altar fire be kept burning perpetually and that incense be offered with coals taken from the altar); (b) an incense offering at an unauthorized time (outside the prescribed morning and evening hours); or (c) an unauthorized approach into the inner sanctuary beyond the appointed limits. The LXX renders esh zarah as πῦρ ἀλλότριον (pyr allotrion, "strange/alien/foreign fire"), using ἀλλότριος (allotrios, "belonging to another / foreign / unauthorized"). Whatever the specific act, the text's judgment is unambiguous: it is fire "which he had not commanded them" (asher lo' tsivvah otam) — authorization by divine command, not priestly initiative or sincerity, is the criterion of legitimate worship in the Levitical system] — which he had not commanded them [asher lo' tsivvah otam].

**2** And fire came out from before the LORD [vattetse esh mi-lifney YHWH] [vattetse esh mi-lifney YHWH — "and fire came out from before the LORD"; the exact same phrase used at Lev 9:24 of the divine fire that consumed the legitimate offerings, now repeated with devastating irony: the same divine fire that accepted the authorized worship of the installed priests now destroys two of those priests for unauthorized worship; the fire from God's presence is not tame or merely favorable — it is holy, which means it both accepts what is offered rightly and destroys what approaches wrongly; compare Num 16:35 (fire consumes Korach's 250 men) and Heb 12:29 ("our God is a consuming fire")] and consumed them [vattokal otam] [vattokal — from akhal, "to eat/consume"; the same verb used of the divine fire "consuming" the offerings at 9:24 — the fire does not merely kill Nadav and Avihu, it "eats" them as it ate the burnt offering; the verbal echo is exact and intentional: the sacred fire makes no distinction between offerings and offerers when the offense is against the holy] and they died before the LORD [vayyamutu lifney YHWH].

**3** And Moshe said to Aharon [vayomer Moshe el-Aharon]: "This is what the LORD spoke [hu' asher dibber YHWH] saying [lemor]: 'Among those near me I will show myself holy [bi-qerovai eqqadesh] [bi-qerovai eqqadesh — "among those near me I will show myself holy"; a dense, difficult saying. The key terms: (a) bi-qerovai — "among those who draw near to me / among my near ones"; from qrv, the root of qorban ("offering") and qarov ("near"); those who approach the LORD in the priestly service are the qerovim, the "near ones"; (b) eqqadesh — niphal of qdsh, "to be holy / to show oneself holy / to be sanctified / to be treated as holy"; the niphal can be reflexive ("I will sanctify myself"), tolerative ("I will allow myself to be treated as holy"), or resultative ("holiness will be demonstrated"). The verse is a divine assertion that the LORD's holiness will be manifested precisely at the point of encounter with those who serve him most closely — the priests; when the boundary is violated, the LORD's holiness manifests as consuming judgment. The saying appears to be a pre-existing divine word (asher dibber YHWH, "what the LORD spoke"), possibly given during the Sinai revelation but not previously recorded in the narrative — Moshe cites it as already authoritative. The LXX renders eqqadesh as ἁγιασθήσομαι (hagiasthēsomai, "I will be sanctified / I will show myself holy"), using the future passive of ἁγιάζω (hagiazō)] and before all the people I will be glorified [ve-al-pney kol-ha-am ekkabed]'" [ekkabed — niphal of kbd, "to be heavy / to be honored / to be glorified"; the root of kavod ("glory/honor"); the LORD's glory will be publicly displayed even — especially — through the judgment of his own priests: the holiness of the sanctuary is not a negotiable quality, and its enforcement before the entire people constitutes the revelation of divine glory; compare Ezek 28:22; 38:23, where the same verb ekkabed (LXX: δοξασθήσομαι, doxasthēsomai, "I will be glorified") accompanies divine judgment that reveals the LORD's nature to the watching nations].

And Aharon was silent [va-yiddom Aharon] [va-yiddom Aharon — "and Aharon was silent / and Aharon became still"; from dumah / damam, "to be silent, to be still, to be speechless"; the word connotes not merely absence of speech but the stunned, controlled silence of one who receives devastating news and holds it — the same root appears in Ps 22:2 (the silence of God), Ps 39:2-3 (deliberate silence before the wicked), and Lam 3:26 ("it is good to wait quietly [dumam] for the salvation of the LORD"). Aharon's silence is one of the most charged moments in all of Leviticus. He has just watched two of his four sons be consumed by divine fire. He says nothing. Some commentators read this as numbness; others as holy acceptance; others as the restraint of a priest who understands that the divine holiness has acted rightly even when at incalculable personal cost. The narrative does not explain the silence — it simply records it, in two Hebrew words, and moves on. The LXX renders va-yiddom as καὶ κατενύχθη Ααρων (kai katenychthē Aarōn, "and Aaron was pierced / was struck with compunction"), using κατανύσσω (katanyssō, "to pierce, to be cut to the heart") — a significantly more emotionally interpretive rendering than the MT's blank "was silent"].

---

**4** And Moshe called to Mishael [vayiqra Moshe el-Mishael] and to Eltsaphan [ve-el-Eltsaphan] [Mishael and Eltsaphan — sons of Uzziel, who was the son of Qehath, who was the son of Levi (Ex 6:18, 22); they are first cousins of Aharon, and thus Levites of the Qehatite clan — the clan charged with carrying the most sacred objects of the tabernacle (Num 4:4-20); the Qehatites are the priestly-adjacent Levites, closely associated with the sanctuary service; Moshe turns to them because Aharon's remaining sons (Eleazar and Itamar) are still bound by their priestly duties and because Aharon's family has been forbidden to mourn (v.6)] — sons of Uzziel [bney-Uzziel] — the uncle of Aharon [dod Aharon] — and he said to them [vayomer alehem]: "Draw near [qirvu] and carry your brothers [se'u et-acheykhem] from before the holy place [mi-lifney ha-qodesh] to the outside of the camp [el-michutz la-machaneh]."

**5** And they drew near [vayiqrevu] and carried them in their tunics [vayissa'um be-kuttonotam] [be-kuttonotam — "in their tunics"; the tunic (kuttonet) is the basic priestly garment — the linen undergarment worn next to the body (Lev 8:7, 13); the text specifies that the bodies are carried in their tunics, suggesting that the tunics have somehow survived the fire or that the fire consumed the persons from within while leaving the outer garments partially intact; some commentators note that the divine fire "consumed" (vattokal) Nadav and Avihu, which may describe an interior burning that killed without entirely incinerating; the phrase may also indicate that the bodies were carried out reverently, without being stripped or handled beyond necessity] to the outside of the camp [el-michutz la-machaneh] — as Moshe had spoken [ka'asher dibber Moshe].

---

**6** And Moshe said to Aharon [vayomer Moshe el-Aharon] and to Eleazar [ve-el-Eleazar] and to Itamar [ve-el-Itamar] — his sons [banav]: "Your heads you shall not uncover [rosheykhem lo' tifrau] [tifrau — from para', "to uncover / to loosen / to let go wild"; specifically of the hair: "do not let your hair hang loose / do not dishevel your hair"; in the ancient Near East, disheveled hair was a sign of mourning — lacerating the body, tearing garments, and loosening or shaving the hair were all mourning gestures; these are explicitly prohibited for priests in Lev 21:5, 10-11 (especially the high priest); by prohibiting disheveled hair on the day of the deaths of their brothers and sons, Moshe is invoking the priestly mourning-prohibition and applying it even to this extreme personal loss] and your garments you shall not tear [u-vigdeykhem lo' tiqqarau] [tiqqarau — from qara', "to tear"; tearing the outer garment at the neck or chest was the standard grief gesture throughout biblical narrative (Gen 37:29, 34; 44:13; Josh 7:6; 2 Sam 1:11; 13:31; Job 1:20; Joel 2:13); the prohibition to Aharon and his sons is total: on the day of office, priestly duty supersedes even the deepest personal grief; to mourn publicly would be to act as if the divine judgment were a tragedy to be lamented rather than a holy act to be accepted; the LXX renders tiqqarau as διαρρήξετε (diarrēxete, "you shall tear apart"), confirming the garment-tearing custom as the referent] — lest you die [pen-tamutun] and upon all the congregation he be wrathful [ve-al kol-ha-edah yiqtsop] [yiqtsop — from qatsaf, "to be furious / to be wrathful"; the same verb used of the divine wrath at the murmuring in the wilderness (Num 16:22; 17:11); the warning is not merely personal — if Aharon and his sons mourn publicly, the divine wrath will fall not only on them but upon the entire congregation; the holiness of the priestly office in the sanctuary carries communal weight: the priest's conduct before the LORD affects the whole community he represents] — but your brothers [va-acheykhem] — all the house of Yisra'el [kol beyt Yisra'el] — they shall weep [yivku] for the burning [al-ha-sreifah] [ha-sreifah — "the burning"; from saraf, "to burn"; a stark, plain noun for what has happened — not a euphemism, not a title, simply "the burning"; the community is permitted, even expected, to mourn the death of the two priests; it is specifically the officiating priests who are prohibited from mourning — their duty before the LORD continues regardless of personal loss] which the LORD has set ablaze [asher saraf YHWH].

**7** "And from the entrance of the tent of meeting [u-mi-petach ohel mo'ed] you shall not go out [lo' tetse'u] lest you die [pen-tamutun] — for the anointing oil of the LORD [ki shemen mishchat YHWH] is upon you [aleykhem]." And they did according to the word of Moshe [vaya'asu ki-devar Moshe] [ki shemen mishchat YHWH aleykhem — "for the anointing oil of the LORD is upon you"; the anointing oil (shemen ha-mishchah) applied to Aharon and his sons at their ordination (Lev 8:30) has consecrated them to a status that carries ongoing obligations; the oil of anointing is not a past event but a present condition — they are, at this moment, anointed priests whose consecration must not be compromised by the ritual impurity associated with contact with the dead or with mourning gestures; the prohibition to remain at the tent entrance is both a restriction (they cannot leave their post) and a protection (to leave would be to abandon the consecrated space and incur death)].

---

**8** And the LORD spoke to Aharon [vayedabber YHWH el-Aharon] saying [lemor]:

**9** "Wine [yayin] and strong drink [ve-shekhar] [yayin ve-shekhar — "wine and strong drink"; a standard biblical merism for all intoxicating beverages; yayin is fermented grape wine; shekhar ("strong drink") is a broader term for other fermented beverages — beer, date wine, grain fermentation products; the merism yayin ve-shekhar occurs frequently in legal and prophetic texts as the comprehensive prohibition formula (Num 6:3; Deut 14:26; Judg 13:4, 7, 14; 1 Sam 1:15; Isa 5:11, 22; Prov 20:1; 31:4, 6); the LXX renders yayin ve-shekhar as οἶνον καὶ σίκερα (oinon kai sikera, "wine and strong drink"), transliterating shekhar as sikera — a borrowing that passes into Latin as sicera and into the NT at Luke 1:15 (John the Baptist "shall not drink wine or strong drink" — οἶνον καὶ σίκερα); the Levitical priestly prohibition thus provides the specific vocabulary for the Nazirite prohibition (Num 6) and the Baptist's dedication] you shall not drink [lo' tishteh] — you and your sons with you [attah u-vanekha ittekha] — when you enter [bevaakhem] the tent of meeting [el-ohel mo'ed] — lest you die [ve-lo' tamutun] [ve-lo' tamutun — "lest you die"; the triple death-warning in this chapter (vv.6, 7, 9) is remarkable — on the very day two priests have died by divine action, the LORD issues three new prohibitions each carrying the death penalty; the chapter's rhetorical structure uses the deaths of Nadav and Avihu as the paradigmatic demonstration of what happens when priestly boundaries are violated, then immediately legislates the specific rules whose violation could produce the same result]; it is a statute forever [chuqqat olam] [chuqqat olam — "a perpetual statute / a statute for ever"; chuqqah is a category of divine decree whose rationale is not given — it is authoritative by virtue of divine command, not by virtue of explained reasoning; olam means "forever / for all time / into the distant future"; the alcohol prohibition during priestly service is a chuqqat olam — it admits no exceptions and no expiration] throughout your generations [le-dorotekhem]:

**10** "to distinguish [le-havdil] between the holy and the common [beyn ha-qodesh u-veyn ha-chol] [beyn ha-qodesh u-veyn ha-chol — "between the holy and the common/profane"; the verb havdil (hiphil of badal, "to separate, distinguish, divide") is the same root used at Creation for the separation of light from darkness (Gen 1:4, 6, 7, 14, 18) and for the Levitical separation of clean from unclean animals (Lev 11:47); the priestly vocation is fundamentally a vocation of distinction-making — maintaining the boundary between what belongs to the LORD (qodesh, "holy") and what belongs to ordinary life (chol, "common/profane"; from a root meaning "pierced / worn through / without sacred status"); the LXX renders le-havdil beyn ha-qodesh u-veyn ha-chol as διαστεῖλαι ἀνὰ μέσον τῶν ἁγίων καὶ τῶν βεβήλων (diasteilai ana meson tōn hagiōn kai tōn bebēlōn, "to distinguish between the holy things and the profane things"), using διαστέλλω (diastellō, "to separate / to give distinct orders") and βέβηλος (bebēlos, "profane / accessible to all / not sacred") — the latter term is the standard LXX rendering of chol throughout Leviticus and Ezekiel] and between the unclean and the clean [u-veyn ha-tame' u-veyn ha-tahor]:

**11** "and to teach [u-le-horot] [u-le-horot — hiphil infinitive of yarah, "to throw / to point / to direct / to teach"; the hiphil of yarah is the root of Torah (torah) — "instruction, direction, teaching"; the priestly vocation includes not only ritual performance but the teaching function: the priest is the authorized instructor who communicates the divine distinctions to the people; this teaching role is elaborated in Deut 17:9-11; 24:8; 31:9-13; Ezek 44:23; Mal 2:6-7; Heb 5:12; the connection between sobriety and accurate teaching is explicit: the alcohol prohibition directly precedes the instruction mandate, implying that intoxication compromises the cognitive and moral precision required for priestly teaching] the sons of Yisra'el [et-bney Yisra'el] all the statutes [et kol-ha-chuqqim] which the LORD has spoken to them [asher dibber YHWH alehem] through Moshe [be-yad Moshe] [be-yad Moshe — "by the hand of Moshe"; the standard Hebrew idiom for prophetic/Mosaic mediation — the LORD speaks, Moshe is the instrument ("hand") through which the divine word reaches the people; LXX: διὰ χειρὸς Μωυσῆ (dia cheiros Mōusē, "through the hand of Moses")].

---

**12** And Moshe spoke to Aharon [vayedabber Moshe el-Aharon] and to Eleazar his son [ve-el-Eleazar beno] and to Itamar his son [ve-el-Itamar beno] — the ones remaining [ha-notarim] [ha-notarim — "the remaining ones / the survivors"; from yatar, "to remain, to be left over"; the word is weighty: Eleazar and Itamar are now "the remaining ones" — the ones left after the death of Nadav and Avihu; the term quietly acknowledges the catastrophic reduction of the priestly family without dwelling on it]: "Take the grain offering [qechu et-ha-minchah] that remains [ha-noteret] from the fire offerings of the LORD [me-ishey YHWH] [ishey YHWH — "fire offerings of the LORD / offerings by fire to the LORD"; from esh ("fire") + the relational termination; the term encompasses all offerings consumed by fire on the altar; some scholars (following BDB) connect isheh to a root meaning "gift/offering" rather than "fire," but the fire-connection is supported by context throughout Leviticus] and eat it [ve-ikhlukha] unleavened [matstsot] beside the altar [etsel ha-mizbeach] — for it is most holy [ki qodesh qodashim hi'] [qodesh qodashim — "most holy / holy of holies"; the superlative construction (qodesh + qodashim genitive) = "holiness of holinesses" — the highest degree of holiness in the Levitical system; objects and portions designated qodesh qodashim include: the shewbread (Lev 24:9), the incense (Ex 30:36), the guilt offering (Lev 14:13), the sin offering (Lev 6:25), and the grain offering (Lev 2:3, 10; 6:17); the "most holy" status means the item may be eaten only by male priests, only in the court of the tabernacle, and only in the same day].

**13** "And you shall eat it [ve-akhaltem otah] in a holy place [be-maqom qadosh] — for it is your due [ki chaqekha ve-chaq-banekha hi'] [chaqekha ve-chaq-banekha — "your appointed-portion and the appointed-portion of your sons"; from chaqaq, "to inscribe / to appoint / to decree"; the chaq is not merely a customary share but a legally decreed portion — inscribed by divine decree as belonging to the priests; the same root appears in chuqqat olam ("perpetual statute") at v.9; the priestly portion of the most holy offerings is as fixed as any divine statute] and the due of your sons [ve-chaq-banekha] — from the fire offerings of the LORD [min-ishey YHWH] — for thus I have been commanded [ki ken tsuvveyti] [ki ken tsuvveyti — "for so I have been commanded"; Moshe speaks in first person, acknowledging his own position as one under divine command; the humility of the construction is notable — even Moshe does not claim to originate the priestly law but presents himself as commanded by the LORD, the same Commander who commands the priests].

**14** "And the breast of the wave offering [ve-et chazeh ha-tenufah] [chazeh ha-tenufah — "the breast of the wave offering"; the tenufah ("wave offering / elevation offering") is a ritual gesture in which a portion is held and moved before the LORD — waved horizontally (as distinguished from the vertical terumah gesture); the breast (chazeh) of the peace offerings is designated the priestly portion by the wave-offering rite (Lev 7:30-31); it is a lesser degree of holiness than the qodesh qodashim portions — it may be eaten anywhere in a clean place, by all members of the priestly household including women and children] and the thigh of the contribution [ve-et shok ha-terumah] [shok ha-terumah — "the thigh of the contribution / heave-offering"; the terumah is a "raised" portion, separated and given to the LORD and thence to the priest; the shok (leg/thigh) is the priestly portion of the peace offering, given to the officiating priest (Lev 7:32-34); together, chazeh and shok constitute the two priestly portions from every shelamim offering: the breast by wave-offering, the right thigh by terumah; these are "lesser holy" (qodesh qatan) rather than "most holy" (qodesh qodashim) portions] you shall eat in a clean place [tokhelu be-maqom tahor] — you and your sons and your daughters with you [attah u-vanekha u-venotekha ittekha] — for as your due and the due of your sons they have been given [ki chaqekha ve-chaq-banekha nitnah] from the sacrifices of the peace offerings of the sons of Yisra'el [mi-zivchey shelamey bney Yisra'el].

**15** "The thigh of the contribution [shok ha-terumah] and the breast of the wave offering [ve-chazeh ha-tenufah] — upon the fire offerings of the fats [al ishey ha-chalavim] they shall bring [yavi'u] to wave [le-hanif] as a wave offering [tenufah] before the LORD [lifney YHWH] — and it shall be for you [ve-hayah lekha] and for your sons with you [u-le-vanekha ittekha] as a due forever [le-choq olam] — as the LORD commanded [ka'asher tsivvah YHWH]."

---

**16** And Moshe diligently sought [u-vaqesh biqesh Moshe] [u-vaqesh biqesh Moshe — "and Moshe sought, he sought"; the doubling of the verb (biqesh biqesh) is an intensive construction in Biblical Hebrew — "he searched carefully / he inquired insistently"; the doubled verb signals urgency and thoroughness; the same emphatic construction appears at Gen 43:7; the searching is for the goat of the sin offering — Moshe wants to know what has become of it, and when he cannot find it, he investigates] the goat of the sin offering [et-se'ir ha-chattat] and behold [ve-hinneh] — it had been burned [saraf] [saraf — "he burned it" / "it was burned"; the verb is unspecific about subject — either Eleazar and Itamar burned it deliberately, or it is a narrative statement of condition (it had been burned up); given Moshe's anger (v.16) and Aharon's defense (vv.19-20), the burning was an act by the priests themselves, presumably on the grounds stated by Aharon in v.19]. And he was angry [vayyiqtsof] at Eleazar [al-Eleazar] and at Itamar [ve-al-Itamar] — the sons of Aharon [bney Aharon] — the remaining ones [ha-notarim] [ha-notarim — "the surviving ones"; again the heavy word of v.12 reappears; Moshe's anger is directed at the surviving sons, the ones who performed the service]: saying [lemor]:

**17** "Why [maddua'] did you not eat the sin offering [lo' akhaltem et-ha-chattat] in the holy place [ba-maqom ha-qodesh] [ba-maqom ha-qodesh — "in the holy place"; the requirement that the sin offering be eaten by the priests in the courtyard of the tabernacle is given in Lev 6:26 — the sin offering whose blood is not brought into the inner sanctuary is to be eaten by the male priests in the court; eating the sin offering in the holy place is not merely a priestly privilege but a mandatory ritual act that is part of the atonement procedure]? For it is most holy [ki qodesh qodashim hi'] and he gave it to you [ve-otah natan lakhem] to bear the iniquity of the congregation [la-set et-avon ha-edah] [la-set et-avon ha-edah — "to carry / bear the iniquity of the congregation"; the verb nasa' ("to bear / to carry / to lift") is the same root used for the scapegoat carrying the sins of Yisra'el on the Day of Atonement (Lev 16:22) and for the Suffering Servant bearing iniquities (Isa 53:11-12); the priests' eating of the sin offering is not merely dietary — it is a priestly act of bearing and removing the people's sin; the sin offering confers atonement not only through the blood manipulation but through the eating of the flesh by the officiating priests, who thereby "absorb" the sin from the offering into themselves and carry it out of the sacred sphere; Moshe's anger is therefore not about a technicality but about the incompletion of an atonement act] to make atonement for them [le-khapper alehem] before the LORD [lifney YHWH]?

**18** "Behold [hen] — its blood was not brought [lo' huva et-damo] into the inner sanctuary [el-ha-qodesh] [el-ha-qodesh — "into the holy [place] / into the inner sanctuary"; here referring to the inner precincts of the tabernacle (the tent itself) as distinguished from the outer courtyard; the rule of Lev 6:30 states that any sin offering whose blood is brought "into the tent of meeting to make atonement in the holy place" must be burned and not eaten; the negative confirmation here — "its blood was not brought into the inner sanctuary" — establishes that this was not one of those cases; therefore the normal rule applied: the priests were to eat it in the courtyard]. In the holy place [ba-qodesh] — you certainly should have eaten it [akol tokhal otah] — as I commanded [ka'asher tsivveyti]."

**19** And Aharon spoke to Moshe [vayedabber Aharon el-Moshe] [vayedabber Aharon el-Moshe — "and Aharon spoke to Moshe"; this is Aharon's first speech in all of chapter 10 — his only words after the silence of v.3; the contrast is deliberate and devastating: Aharon is silent at the death of his sons (v.3), but he speaks to defend his surviving sons from Moshe's anger (v.19); the speech is brief, restrained, and theologically profound]: "Behold [hen] — today [ha-yom] they brought near their sin offering [hiqrivu et-chattattam] and their burnt offering [ve-et-olatam] before the LORD [lifney YHWH] and things like these have happened to me [vattiqqrenah otiy ka-eleh] [vattiqqrenah otiy ka-eleh — "and things like these have happened to me / and such things have befallen me"; from qarah, "to meet / to happen / to befall"; the phrase is a restrained understatement for the catastrophe of the day — two of Aharon's sons have been killed by divine fire; Aharon refers to the deaths of Nadav and Avihu without naming them or describing them, by the phrase "things like these" (ka-eleh); the restraint is almost unbearable in its compression]. And if I had eaten [ve-akhalti] the sin offering today [chattat ha-yom] — would it have been acceptable in the eyes of the LORD [ha-yitav be-eyney YHWH] [ha-yitav be-eyney YHWH — "would it have been good/pleasing in the eyes of the LORD?"; the rhetorical question implies that eating the sin offering in the shadow of such a bereavement would not have been ritually appropriate — Aharon suggests that the eating by priests in mourning-proximity to the dead would compromise rather than complete the atonement; his argument is that the extraordinary circumstances of this day — the deaths, the explicit mourning prohibition that yet leaves the inner grief intact — made the eating of the sin offering ritually impossible or inappropriate; the argument touches the tension between the written law (eat the sin offering) and the spirit of the priestly law (the eating must constitute genuine bearing of the people's sin before a holy God, which a priest in such extremity cannot authentically perform)]?"

**20** And Moshe heard [vayyishma' Moshe] and it was acceptable in his eyes [vayyitav be-eynav] [vayyitav be-eynav — "and it was good in his eyes / it pleased him / it seemed right to him"; the verb yatav (hiphil or qal) means "to be good, to be pleasing, to seem right"; the identical vocabulary echoes Aharon's question in v.19 (ha-yitav be-eyney YHWH — "would it have been good in the eyes of the LORD?") and now answers it: Moshe finds Aharon's defense good; and implicitly, because Moshe acts as the LORD's authorized spokesman throughout Leviticus, Moshe's acceptance stands in for divine acceptance; the chapter ends with no further divine word — no condemnation, no explicit endorsement; just Moshe hearing, and the matter being acceptable; the resolution is deliberately understated, matching the tone of Aharon's silence and his restrained speech].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Leningrad Codex B19A, Folios 63r–63v)

1. **v.1 — Esh zarah / "Strange fire":** The phrase אֵשׁ זָרָה (esh zarah, "strange/unauthorized fire") is the theological hinge of the entire chapter and one of the most discussed locutions in Leviticus. The Leningrad Codex reads it plainly, without textual variant in the MT tradition. The word זָרָה (zarah, "strange/foreign/unauthorized") derives from the root זוּר (zur), which appears throughout the priestly legislation in the noun זָר (zar, "outsider / unauthorized person") — one who is not of the Aaronide priestly line and therefore prohibited from performing altar service (Num 1:51; 3:10, 38; 16:40; 18:4, 7). What makes the offense of Nadav and Avihu particularly acute is that they are not zar in the sense of being outside the priestly line — they are Aharon's sons, installed priests — yet they offer esh zarah, "unauthorized-person's fire," fire of the kind that belongs to the unauthorized. The offense is not one of rank but of authorization: they act outside the specific divine command (asher lo' tsivvah otam, "which he had not commanded them"). The phrase lo' tsivvah ("had not commanded") is the negative counterpart to the compliance formula ka'asher tsivvah YHWH ("as the LORD commanded") that recurs like a refrain through Lev 8-9 (8:4, 9, 13, 17, 21, 29; 9:7, 10) — eight times the phrase affirms compliant action; now, at the chapter's opening, the formula appears in its negative: action was taken where the LORD had not commanded.

2. **v.2 — Narrative symmetry with 9:24:** The verbal echo between 9:24 and 10:2 is one of the most precise literary-theological constructions in the Torah. Compare:

    - Lev 9:24: וַתֵּצֵא אֵשׁ מִלִּפְנֵי יְהוָה וַתֹּאכַל עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ (vattetse esh mi-lifney YHWH vattokal al-ha-mizbeach, "and fire came out from before the LORD and consumed upon the altar")
    - Lev 10:2: וַתֵּצֵא אֵשׁ מִלִּפְנֵי יְהוָה וַתֹּאכַל אוֹתָם (vattetse esh mi-lifney YHWH vattokal otam, "and fire came out from before the LORD and consumed them")

    The only difference is the object of vattokal: in 9:24, the fire consumes "upon the altar" (al-ha-mizbeach — the offerings); in 10:2, the fire consumes "them" (otam — Nadav and Avihu). The divine fire is identical in origin and action; what changes is what it meets. The altar-offerings of chapter 9 were presented according to divine command and are consumed in acceptance. The esh zarah of 10:1 is presented without divine command and is met by the same divine fire — which then consumes not the offering but the offerers. The fire does not distinguish on the basis of identity (they are priests) but on the basis of authorization (their fire was not commanded). The LXX renders both passages with the same Greek: καὶ ἐξῆλθεν πῦρ παρὰ κυρίου (kai exēlthen pyr para kyriou), preserving the verbal echo.

3. **v.3 — Va-yiddom Aharon / "And Aharon was silent":** The silence of Aharon is one of the most commented passages in Jewish exegetical tradition. The Talmud (Zevachim 115b) reads it as voluntary restraint receiving divine reward — because Aharon was silent, he received the direct divine speech of vv.8-11 as a reward, the only portion of the Levitical legal material addressed to Aharon alone (rather than to Moshe for relay). Nachmanides (Ramban) reads the silence as holy submission to divine judgment. Philo interprets it as philosophical mastery of emotion by reason. Modern commentators (Milgrom, Levine, Alter) note the literary power of the unadorned statement — the Leningrad Codex gives just four Hebrew words (va-yiddom Aharon) with no gloss, no explanation, no report of what Aharon felt. The text trusts silence to carry more than speech could. The LXX's interpretive addition (κατενύχθη, "was pierced / struck with compunction") represents the Greek translators' attempt to fill the silence with emotional content — and in doing so, diminishes the raw power of the Hebrew's restraint.

4. **vv.8-11 — The alcohol prohibition as interpretive key to Nadav and Avihu:** The placement of the wine-and-strong-drink prohibition immediately after the incident and mourning prohibition (vv.1-7) has been read since antiquity as an implicit explanation of the esh zarah offense: Nadav and Avihu may have approached the LORD while intoxicated. This reading, endorsed by Talmud Sanhedrin 38b, Vayikra Rabbah 12:1, Philo, and many patristic sources (e.g., Novatian, De Cibis Iudaicis), gains support from the structural principle of biblical narrative by which a law given immediately after an incident often implies that the law is responsive to the incident. The placement of vv.8-11 is the only material in Leviticus 1-16 addressed directly to Aharon (rather than through Moshe), and it comes on the very day of the incident. However, the text itself does not say Nadav and Avihu were drunk — the offense is stated as esh zarah / asher lo' tsivvah otam, without mention of intoxication. The alcohol prohibition may be responsive to the incident without being explanatory of it — the LORD may be issuing additional protective legislation in light of what has just occurred, ensuring that the conditions for future unauthorized approaches are minimized.

5. **v.10 — Beyn ha-qodesh u-veyn ha-chol / the priestly vocation of distinction:** The paired distinctions of v.10 — qodesh/chol (holy/common) and tame'/tahor (unclean/clean) — represent the two great classificatory axes of the Levitical system. The qodesh/chol axis concerns proximity to the divine: things designated qodesh belong to the sphere of the LORD's presence and must be treated accordingly; chol things belong to ordinary life. The tame'/tahor axis concerns ritual status: tahor ("clean") objects and persons may enter the sacred sphere; tame' ("unclean") ones may not. These two axes are not identical: a thing can be qodesh but tame' (e.g., a holy vessel that has contracted impurity), or tahor but chol (e.g., a clean layperson). The priestly task is to maintain and teach both distinctions simultaneously. Ezekiel 44:23 applies precisely this formulation to the restored priests: "they shall teach my people the difference between the holy and the common (beyn qodesh le-chol), and cause them to discern between the unclean and the clean (u-veyn tame' le-tahor)." Malachi 2:6-7 makes the teaching of these distinctions the core definition of the priest as "messenger of the LORD of hosts." The NT applies the concept of priestly distinction-making to the entire people of God (1 Pet 2:9; 1 Cor 6:19-20; 2 Cor 6:14-18).

6. **vv.16-20 — The uneaten sin offering: the legal crisis resolved by grief:** The incident of the uneaten chattat goat (vv.16-20) is the chapter's third crisis, following the deaths of Nadav and Avihu and the mourning prohibition. Moshe's anger at Eleazar and Itamar for not eating the sin offering is legally grounded: the rule of Lev 6:26 requires the officiating priest to eat the sin offering in the court of the tabernacle. This eating is not optional but constitutive of the atonement procedure — the priestly consumption of the flesh is the sacral act by which the priest "bears" (nasa') the people's sin. The goat in question is that of Lev 9:15 — the chattat offered on the eighth day for the people. Its blood was not brought into the inner sanctuary (v.18), so the normal eating rule applied. Yet Eleazar and Itamar burned it rather than ate it. Aharon's defense (v.19) is remarkable for its theological sophistication: he does not dispute the law; he argues that the law's purpose (effective priestly bearing of the congregation's sin before the LORD) could not have been fulfilled on this particular day because of the extremity of what had happened. His argument is implicitly about intention and ritual fitness: a priest in the immediate aftermath of witnessing his sons consumed by divine fire is in a state of internal grief (ovel, mourner) even if prohibited from the external gestures of mourning — and eating the sin offering in such a state might compromise rather than complete the atonement. Moshe accepts this argument (v.20), establishing a precedent for the principle that in extraordinary circumstances, the spirit of the priestly law (genuine, fit bearing of the people's sin) may override the letter (eat the flesh in the court). This precedent enters later halakhic discussion (Talmud Zevachim 100b-101a; Sifra on Lev 10:16).

### LXX (Codex Vaticanus) Observations — Pages 94–95

The Vaticanus text for Lev 10 is largely consistent with the MT, with the following notable divergences and interpretive choices:

- **v.1 — Esh zarah / πῦρ ἀλλότριον:** The LXX renders esh zarah as πῦρ ἀλλότριον (pyr allotrion, "alien/foreign/unauthorized fire"). The Greek ἀλλότριος (allotrios) means "belonging to another, foreign, not one's own" — it captures the "unauthorized" aspect of zarah (the fire is not the fire that belongs to this service) without the relational nuance of the Hebrew zur (the fire of an "outsider"). The LXX adds a clarifying phrase not in the MT: ὃ οὐ προσέταξεν αὐτοῖς κύριος (ho ou prosetaxen autois kyrios, "which the LORD did not order/prescribe for them") — confirming the MT's asher lo' tsivvah otam but making the LORD the explicit subject of the prohibition.

- **v.3 — Va-yiddom / κατενύχθη:** As noted in the translation, the LXX renders va-yiddom Aharon ("and Aharon was silent") as καὶ κατενύχθη Ααρών (kai katenychthē Aarōn, "and Aaron was pierced/struck with compunction"), using the aorist passive of κατανύσσω (katanyssō, "to prick / to pierce / to be stabbed with feeling"). This verb appears in the NT at Acts 2:37 (the Jerusalem crowd on Pentecost "were pierced to the heart" — κατενύγησαν τὴν καρδίαν, katenygēsan tēn kardian), suggesting the LXX's emotional reading of Aharon's response influenced NT vocabulary. The LXX divergence here is significant: the MT's dumah/damam vocabulary suggests controlled, imposed silence; the LXX's katanyssō vocabulary suggests emotional devastation. Both are theologically defensible readings of Aharon's inner state; the MT leaves it open while the LXX interprets.

- **v.6 — Lo' tifrau / οὐκ ἀποκιδαρώσετε:** The LXX renders rosheykhem lo' tifrau ("your heads you shall not uncover / let loose") as τὰς κεφαλὰς ὑμῶν οὐκ ἀποκιδαρώσετε (tas kephalas hymōn ouk apoKidarōsete, "you shall not remove your turbans from your heads"), using ἀποκιδαρόω (apokidaroō, "to remove the kidaris / tiara"), a verb formed from κίδαρις (kidaris), the Greek term for the priestly headdress (transliterated from the Hebrew mitsnefet, "turban"; cf. LXX Ex 28:37). The LXX interprets the command specifically as a prohibition on removing the priestly turban — as a sign of mourning, a priest might remove his sacred headcovering; the LXX here is more specific than the MT's para' ("uncover / let loose"), which could refer to any disheveling of the head.

- **v.9 — Shekhar / σίκερα:** The LXX renders shekhar as σίκερα (sikera), a transliteration rather than a translation. This choice by the LXX translators — to transliterate rather than translate — signals that they had no single Greek equivalent for the broad semantic range of shekhar ("strong drink" of any fermented grain or fruit beverage other than grape wine). The transliterated sikera passes into Latin (sicera) and NT Greek (Luke 1:15), becoming the technical term in Christian and later Western theological tradition for the second member of the prohibition pair. The Vaticanus reads σίκερα clearly at this verse.

- **v.16 — U-vaqesh biqesh / ζητῶν ἐξεζήτησεν:** The LXX renders the doubled verb u-vaqesh biqesh Moshe ("and Moshe diligently sought") as ζητῶν ἐξεζήτησεν Μωυσῆς (zētōn exezētēsen Mōusēs, "seeking, Moses sought out thoroughly"), using a participle + aorist compound construction that mirrors the Hebrew's emphatic doubling: ζητῶν (present active participle of ζητέω, "seeking") + ἐξεζήτησεν (aorist of ἐκζητέω, "to seek out / to seek diligently"). The LXX thus captures both the urgency and the thoroughness of Moshe's inquiry.

- **v.19 — Vattiqqrenah otiy ka-eleh / τοιαῦτά μοι συνήντηκεν:** The LXX renders Aharon's understatement vattiqqrenah otiy ka-eleh ("and things like these have happened to me") as καὶ τοιαῦτά μοι συνήντηκεν (kai toiauta moi synēntēken, "and such things have met me / befallen me"), using συναντάω (synantaō, "to meet, to encounter, to befall") — a more neutral verb than the Hebrew qarah. The Greek preserves the restraint of the Hebrew: Aharon does not say "my sons were killed by divine fire" but "such things have befallen me" — the LXX's toiauta ("such things") matches the Hebrew ka-eleh ("things like these") in its deliberate vagueness.

- **v.20 — Vayyitav be-eynav / ἤρεσεν ἐναντίον αὐτοῦ:** The LXX renders vayyitav be-eynav ("it was good in his eyes / it pleased him") as ἤρεσεν ἐναντίον αὐτοῦ (ēresen enantion autou, "it was pleasing before him"), using ἀρέσκω (areskō, "to please, to be acceptable to"). The preposition ἐναντίον (enantion, "before / in the sight of") rather than ἐν ὀφθαλμοῖς (en ophthalmois, "in the eyes of") is a slight interpretive shift — the LXX's phrase sounds more formal/objective ("was found acceptable before him") than the MT's idiomatic "was good in his eyes." The difference is minor but reflects the LXX's general tendency to slightly formalize the anthropomorphic language of the MT.

— EMET
